content:
# Revelation Chapter 10 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 284-285 | **Source Image:** REV_010_page_001.jpg, REV_010_page_002.jpg

### The Mighty Angel and the Little Scroll

1. Then I saw another mighty angel coming down from heaven. He was wrapped in a cloud, with a rainbow circling his head. His face blazed like the sun, and his legs were like pillars of fire.

2. He held a small scroll that lay open in his hand. He planted his right foot on the sea and his left foot on the land,

3. and he shouted with a voice as powerful as a lion's roar. When he shouted, the seven thunders responded with their own voices.

4. When the seven thunders had spoken, I was about to write down what they said. But a voice from heaven told me, "Seal up what the seven thunders said, and do not write it down."

5. Then the angel I had seen standing on the sea and on the land raised his right hand toward heaven.

6. He swore an oath by the One who lives forever and ever, who created heaven and everything in it, the earth and everything in it, and the sea and everything in it. He declared, "There will be no more delay!

7. When the seventh angel blows his trumpet, God's hidden plan will be fulfilled, just as he announced to his servants the prophets."

8. Then the voice from heaven spoke to me again and said, "Go and take the open scroll from the hand of the angel who is standing on the sea and on the land."

9. So I went to the angel and asked him to give me the small scroll. He said to me, "Take it and eat it. It will turn your stomach sour, but in your mouth it will taste as sweet as honey."

10. I took the small scroll from the angel's hand and ate it. It was as sweet as honey in my mouth, but when I swallowed it, my stomach turned bitter.

11. Then I was told, "You must prophesy again about many peoples, nations, languages, and kings."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 1 — "wrapped in a cloud":** The Sinaiticus text reads περιβεβλημενον νεφελην — "clothed with a cloud." This angel's description echoes theophanies throughout Scripture (the rainbow, the fiery pillars, the sun-like face), suggesting this messenger carries extraordinary divine authority.

**Verse 3 — "responded with their own voices":** The seven thunders (αι επτα βρονται) are presented in the Sinaiticus as having their own articulate speech. The dynamic rendering "responded" captures the dialogic quality — the thunders are not mere echoes but independent speakers answering the angel's cry.

**Verse 6 — "There will be no more delay":** The Greek (χρονος ουκετι εσται) has been traditionally rendered "there will be time no longer," which can confuse modern readers into thinking time itself ceases. The Sinaiticus context makes clear this is about the end of waiting — God's plan will no longer be postponed.

**Verses 9-10 — Sweet and bitter scroll:** The experience of eating the scroll — sweet in the mouth, bitter in the stomach — is a powerful metaphor for prophetic calling. The message of God is delightful to receive but painful to deliver, especially when it contains judgment. This echoes Ezekiel's similar experience (Ezek 3:1-3).

**Verse 11 — "about many peoples, nations, languages, and kings":** The Sinaiticus reads επι λαοις και εθνεσιν και γλωσσαις και βασιλευσιν. The preposition επι here means "concerning" or "about" — John's prophetic commission is being renewed and expanded to encompass the whole world.

— PNEUMA
